ingredients.definition: Beef — meat from cattle.

common_sources.name: Non-halal commercial slaughter
common_sources.note: Generally considered haram or mushbooh by most schools

halal.explanation: Beef is permissible only if the animal was slaughtered according to Islamic requirements (dhabihah). Beef on a generic product label does not specify slaughter method; treat as mushbooh unless the product is certified halal.

ingredients.definition: Cheese is a dairy product made by coagulating milk proteins with an enzyme (rennet) or acid, then aging the resulting curd.

common_sources.name: Microbial rennet (modern, most common in EU)

halal.explanation: Cheese is dairy (halal as a class), but the rennet used to coagulate the milk can be animal-derived. If the rennet came from a non-halal-slaughtered animal, the cheese is mushbooh. Most EU cheese now uses microbial or vegetable rennet (halal); the label rarely says which. When the source is unspecified, verify with the manufacturer.

ingredients.definition: Chicken — meat from domestic chicken or other poultry.

common_sources.name: Non-halal commercial slaughter
common_sources.note: Generally considered haram or mushbooh by most schools

common_sources.name: Mechanically slaughtered
common_sources.note: Disputed; some authorities permit, others reject

halal.explanation: Chicken is permissible only if slaughtered according to Islamic requirements (dhabihah). Generic chicken on a product label does not specify slaughter method; treat as mushbooh unless the product is certified halal.

ingredients.definition: Curcumin (E100) is a bright yellow-orange colorant extracted from the rhizomes of turmeric (Curcuma longa). Used in curry, mustard, butter, cheese, and confectionery.

halal.explanation: Plant-derived natural colorant. Halal under all schools.

ingredients.definition: Cochineal (E120) is a bright red colorant produced by crushing and processing dried cochineal insects.

halal.explanation: Insect-derived ingredients are treated differently by different schools. Some conservative scholars consider all insects (other than locusts) impermissible; others permit insect-derived substances. Classified as mushbooh with disagreement flagged.

ingredients.definition: Caramel (E150) is a brown food coloring produced by heating sugars (sucrose, glucose, or fructose). The four sub-types (E150a-d) differ by the production aid used. Used in cola, sauces, gravies, baked goods, and confectionery.

common_sources.name: Ammonia caramel (E150c)
common_sources.note: Halal — ammonia is processing aid, not in final product

halal.explanation: All four caramel sub-types are produced from plant sugars. The processing aids (caustic, sulfite, ammonia) are not ingredients in the final product. Halal under standard production.

ingredients.definition: Carotenes (E160a) is a natural orange-yellow colorant. Found in carrots, palm oil, algae, and many fruits and vegetables. Used in margarine, butter, dairy, and beverages.

halal.explanation: Plant-derived or synthetically produced. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Annatto (E160b) is a natural orange-red colorant extracted from the seeds of the achiote tree (Bixa orellana). Common in cheese, butter, snack foods, and rice.

halal.explanation: Plant-derived natural colorant. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Paprika Extract (E160c) is a red-orange colorant extracted from red bell peppers. Used in sauces, snacks, cheese, and processed meats.

halal.explanation: Plant-derived. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Beetroot Red (E162) is a purple-red colorant extracted from beetroot (Beta vulgaris). Used in yogurts, ice cream, sausages, and confectionery.

halal.explanation: Plant-derived. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Sorbic Acid (E200) is a preservative that inhibits mold, yeast, and fungi. Used in cheese, dried fruits, baked goods, wines, and dressings.

common_sources.name: Synthetic from petrochemicals or microbial fermentation

halal.explanation: Synthetically produced. No animal or alcohol involvement. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Potassium Sorbate (E202) is the potassium salt of sorbic acid (E200). A preservative against mold and yeast. Common in wine, dried meats, cheese, dips, and baked goods.

halal.explanation: Synthetically produced. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Sodium Benzoate (E211) is a common preservative against bacteria, yeast, and fungi. Found in soft drinks, fruit juices, salad dressings, and pickles.

halal.explanation: Synthetically produced. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Sulfur Dioxide (E220) is a preservative and antioxidant. Used in dried fruits, fruit juices, vinegars, wines, and processed potatoes.

halal.explanation: The additive itself is halal. Note that some products that use sulfur dioxide (e.g. wine) are themselves haram for other reasons — sulfur dioxide in dried fruits, juices, or vinegar does not make those products haram.

ingredients.definition: Lactic Acid (E270) is an organic acid found naturally in fermented foods and produced commercially by bacterial fermentation. Used as an acidifier and preservative in beverages, dairy, pickles, and cured meats.

common_sources.name: Plant carbohydrate fermentation (corn, sugar — modern standard)

halal.explanation: The 'lactic' name is misleading — commercial lactic acid is produced by bacterial fermentation of plant sugars, not extracted from milk. The bacteria are grown on plant or whey substrates; both are halal.

ingredients.definition: Ascorbic Acid (E300) — vitamin C. Used as an antioxidant in fruit juices, cured meats, and baked goods, and as a dough conditioner.

halal.explanation: Commercial ascorbic acid is produced by microbial fermentation of glucose (typically from corn). No animal or alcohol involvement. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Sodium Ascorbate (E301) is the sodium salt of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C). Used as an antioxidant in processed meats, baked goods, and beverages.

halal.explanation: Same source as E300 (ascorbic acid). Halal.

ingredients.definition: Tocopherols (E306) is a natural antioxidant (Vitamin E). Commercial sources are mostly vegetable-oil derived (soybean, sunflower); rarely from animal fat.

halal.explanation: Most commercial tocopherols are vegetable-derived (halal), but animal-fat-derived tocopherols also exist. The label rarely declares the source; treat as mushbooh until verified.

ingredients.definition: Citric Acid (E330) is an organic acid found naturally in citrus fruits. Commercially produced by mold fermentation of carbohydrates. Used as an acidifier and preservative in soft drinks, candy, and processed foods.

common_sources.name: Aspergillus niger mold fermentation of corn syrup or sugar

halal.explanation: Citric acid is produced by mold fermentation of plant carbohydrates (typically corn). Halal under standard production.

ingredients.definition: Sodium Citrate (E331) is the sodium salt of citric acid. Used as an acidity regulator, emulsifier (in processed cheese), and flavor enhancer.

halal.explanation: Derived from citric acid (E330). Halal.

ingredients.definition: Phosphoric Acid (E338) is an inorganic acid used to acidify foods and beverages. Best known as the acid in cola drinks. Also used in baking powder and processed cheese.

common_sources.name: Synthetic / industrial production from phosphate rock

halal.explanation: Inorganic acid, industrially produced from phosphate rock. No animal or alcohol involvement. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Agar (E406) is a gelling agent extracted from red algae (Gelidium and Gracilaria species). Common alternative to gelatin in vegetarian desserts and Asian cuisine.

halal.explanation: Seaweed-derived. Halal under all schools.

ingredients.definition: Carrageenan (E407) is a thickener and gelling agent extracted from red seaweed. Used in dairy products, plant milks, jellies, dressings, and ice cream.

halal.explanation: Carrageenan is extracted from red seaweed. No animal or alcohol involvement. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Locust Bean Gum (E410) is a thickener extracted from the seeds of the carob tree. Used in ice cream, dairy products, and pet food.

halal.explanation: Plant-derived polysaccharide. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Guar Gum (E412) is a thickener and stabilizer extracted from the seeds of the guar bean. Used in baked goods, sauces, ice cream, and gluten-free flour blends.

halal.explanation: Guar gum is a plant-derived polysaccharide. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Xanthan Gum (E415) is a thickener produced by bacterial fermentation of sugar. Used in salad dressings, sauces, syrups, and gluten-free baking.

common_sources.name: Bacterial fermentation (Xanthomonas campestris) of plant sugars

halal.explanation: Xanthan gum is produced by bacterial fermentation of sugar. The bacteria are grown on plant-based substrates. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Sorbitol (E420) is a sugar alcohol used as a sweetener and humectant. Found naturally in fruits and produced commercially by hydrogenation of corn syrup.

halal.explanation: Plant-derived sugar alcohol. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Glycerin (E422) is a sweet, viscous polyol used as a humectant, sweetener, and solvent. Found in baked goods, low-fat foods, beverages, and confectionery.

halal.explanation: Glycerin can be produced from plant oils (halal), animal fats (mushbooh, haram if porcine), or synthetic petroleum-based processes (halal). The label rarely declares the source. Verify with the manufacturer when in doubt.

ingredients.definition: Pectin (E440) is a natural gelling agent extracted from the cell walls of fruit (especially citrus peel and apple pomace). Used in jams, jellies, marmalades, confectionery, and yogurt.

halal.explanation: Pectin is a plant-derived polysaccharide. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Gelatin (E441) is a protein extracted from animal collagen. Used as a gelling agent in candy, marshmallows, gummies, capsules, yogurt, and many desserts.

common_sources.name: Porcine (pig skin and bone)
common_sources.note: Haram. Most common commercial source in EU and US.

common_sources.name: Bovine (cow hide and bone)
common_sources.note: Halal only if from a halal-slaughtered animal

halal.explanation: Gelatin is classified as mushbooh because the source is rarely declared on the label. The default commercial source in EU and US markets is porcine, which is haram. Bovine and fish gelatin exist but are usually labeled when used. Treat as haram unless the product carries a halal certification.

ingredients.definition: Diphosphates (E450) is a family of inorganic phosphate salts. Used as raising agents in baking powder, emulsifiers in processed cheese, and moisture binders in cured meats.

halal.explanation: Inorganic phosphate salt. No animal involvement. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Cellulose (E460) — refined plant cellulose. Used as a thickener, anti-caking agent, and fiber additive in cheese, ice cream, and processed foods.

halal.explanation: Plant cell-wall material. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Carboxymethyl Cellulose (E466) is a modified cellulose used as a thickener and stabilizer. Found in ice cream, baked goods, gluten-free flours, and toothpaste.

halal.explanation: Modified plant cellulose. Halal.

names: Mono- and Di-glycerides of Fatty Acids
ingredients.definition: Mono- and Diglycerides (E471) is an emulsifier derived from glycerol and fatty acids. Used to blend oils and water in bread, margarine, ice cream, peanut butter, and many processed foods.

common_sources.name: Beef tallow
common_sources.note: Halal only if from a halal-slaughtered animal

halal.explanation: Classified as mushbooh because the source fat is almost never declared on the label. Many manufacturers use plant oils (halal), but animal fats including lard are also used. Verification with the manufacturer is the only way to know.

names: Mono- and Diacetyl Tartaric Acid Esters of Mono- and Diglycerides
ingredients.definition: Mono- and Diacetyl Tartaric Acid Esters of Mono- and Diglycerides (E472e) is a dough conditioner derived from glycerides. Common in industrial bread, rolls, and baked goods.

halal.explanation: Inherits the source-verification concern of E471: the underlying fat may be plant or animal. Classified as mushbooh until the manufacturer confirms a plant source.

ingredients.definition: Polyglycerol Esters of Fatty Acids (E475) is an emulsifier produced from glycerol and fatty acids. Used in cake batters, biscuits, and creamy fillings.

halal.explanation: Like E471 and E472, the fatty acids in E475 can be plant- or animal-derived. The label rarely declares the source. Most manufacturers use plant oils (halal), but verification is recommended.

ingredients.definition: Polyglycerol Polyricinoleate (E476) is an emulsifier used in chocolate (and chocolate-coated products) to reduce viscosity, letting manufacturers use less cocoa butter. Made from castor oil and polyglycerol.

halal.explanation: PGPR is produced exclusively from castor oil (plant) and polyglycerol (plant-derived). Halal under standard commercial production.

ingredients.definition: Sodium Stearoyl Lactylate (E481) is an emulsifier produced from stearic acid and lactic acid. Common in bread, cakes, and creamy fillings.

halal.explanation: Classified as mushbooh because stearic acid can be either plant- or animal-derived and labels do not distinguish. Verify with the manufacturer.

ingredients.definition: Stearyl Tartrate (E483) is a flour treatment agent derived from stearyl alcohol and tartaric acid. Used to improve dough strength in commercial baking.

halal.explanation: Stearyl tartrate is classified as mushbooh because the stearyl alcohol can be derived from either plant or animal sources, and the halal status depends on origin. Verification with the manufacturer or a certification body is recommended.

ingredients.definition: Sodium Bicarbonate (E500) — inorganic salts. E500ii is sodium bicarbonate (baking soda), used as a raising agent and acidity regulator. Found in baked goods, effervescent tablets, and processed foods.

halal.explanation: Mineral salt. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Bone Phosphate (E542) is an anti-caking agent made from animal bone meal. Used in powdered foods, supplements, and toothpaste.

common_sources.name: Porcine bone
common_sources.note: Haram. Common source in EU markets.

common_sources.name: Bovine bone
common_sources.note: Halal only if from a halal-slaughtered animal

halal.explanation: Made directly from animal bones, commonly porcine in EU markets. Even when bovine-sourced, the source animal must have been halal-slaughtered. Treat as haram unless the manufacturer explicitly confirms a halal source.

opinions.note: Source-dependent but typically porcine in EU

ingredients.definition: Silicon Dioxide (E551) is an anti-caking agent. Used in powdered foods (spices, coffee creamer, salt, supplements) to prevent clumping.

halal.explanation: Inorganic mineral. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Glucono Delta-Lactone (E575) is an acidifier produced by fermentation of glucose. Used in baking powders, processed cheese, and tofu coagulation.

halal.explanation: Plant-fermentation product. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Glutamic Acid (E620) is a free amino acid used as a flavor enhancer. The acid form of MSG (E621). Produced by bacterial fermentation of plant sugars.

halal.explanation: Modern production is bacterial fermentation of plant sugars. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Monosodium Glutamate (E621) is a flavor enhancer used to add savory (umami) taste. Originally extracted from seaweed; modern commercial production is by bacterial fermentation of plant sugars.

common_sources.name: Bacterial fermentation of sugar beet molasses or corn starch

halal.explanation: Modern MSG is produced by bacterial fermentation of plant carbohydrates. Halal. (Historical concerns about pig-fat-based fermentation media are obsolete; current commercial production uses plant substrates only.)

ingredients.definition: Disodium Guanylate (E627) is a flavor enhancer commonly paired with MSG. Often labeled as part of the blend E635 (disodium ribonucleotides).

halal.explanation: Like E631, the source can be fish, yeast, or animal (potentially porcine). Modern industrial production favors yeast extract or fermentation (halal), but labels do not specify. Treat as mushbooh until verified.

ingredients.definition: Disodium Inosinate (E631) is a flavor enhancer commonly paired with MSG (E621) to intensify savory taste. Used in chips, instant noodles, and snacks.

halal.explanation: Historically extracted from fish or meat (including pork). Modern production sometimes uses microbial fermentation, which is halal, but labels do not specify the production method. Classified as mushbooh until verified.

ingredients.definition: Disodium Ribonucleotides (E635) is a blend of E627 (disodium guanylate) and E631 (disodium inosinate) used as a flavor enhancer.

halal.explanation: Inherits the source-verification concern of E631. The same blend may be made from fish, meat (including pork), or microbial fermentation. Classified as mushbooh until verified.

ingredients.definition: Shellac (E904) is a natural resin secreted by the female lac insect. Used as a glaze on candies (jelly beans, M&Ms), pharmaceutical pills, and waxed fruit.

halal.explanation: Like E120 carmine, shellac is insect-derived and scholarly opinions differ on its permissibility. Many community lists classify it as mushbooh; conservative scholars advise avoidance. Classified as mushbooh with disagreement flagged.

ingredients.definition: L-Cysteine (E920) is an amino acid used as a dough conditioner to make commercial bread softer and easier to machine. Common in bagels, pizza crust, and packaged bread.

common_sources.name: Duck or chicken feathers
common_sources.note: Mushbooh; halal only if poultry was halal-slaughtered

halal.explanation: Industrial L-cysteine has been extracted from human hair, duck feathers, and synthetic/microbial fermentation. Only synthetic or microbial origin is unambiguously halal. Labels do not declare the source; classified as mushbooh until verified.

opinions.note: Often from human hair or duck feathers

ingredients.definition: Acesulfame K (E950) is a synthetic sweetener about 200 times sweeter than sugar. Used in diet drinks, sugar-free desserts, and tabletop sweeteners.

halal.explanation: Synthetic sweetener. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Aspartame (E951) is a synthetic sweetener about 200 times sweeter than sugar. Made by joining the amino acids aspartic acid and phenylalanine. Common in diet sodas and sugar-free gum.

common_sources.name: Synthetic / chemical synthesis from amino acids

halal.explanation: Aspartame is a synthetic peptide. Halal under all schools.

ingredients.definition: Sucralose (E955) is a synthetic sweetener about 600 times sweeter than sugar, made by chlorinating sucrose. Used in diet beverages, sugar-free baked goods, and tabletop sweeteners.

halal.explanation: Synthetically produced from sugar. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Neotame (E961) is a synthetic sweetener derived from aspartame, about 8000 times sweeter than sugar.

halal.explanation: Synthetic sweetener. Halal.

ingredients.definition: Ethanol is the type of alcohol produced by fermentation and present in alcoholic beverages. Also used industrially as a solvent for flavorings, extracts, and pharmaceuticals.

common_sources.name: Trace amounts in fermented foods
common_sources.note: Disputed; some scholars permit trace amounts from natural fermentation

halal.explanation: Intoxicating alcohol is forbidden. Added ethanol on an ingredient label is generally haram. Scholars differ on trace residues from natural fermentation; the conservative view treats added ethanol as haram regardless of concentration.

ingredients.definition: Flavoring is a generic regulatory term covering any added taste — synthetic or natural, plant or animal, with or without alcohol carrier. The label does not name the underlying ingredients.

halal.explanation: An opaque ingredient category. May be plant-based and halal, or may use alcohol carriers, or rarely may include animal-derived components. Classified as mushbooh by default; verify with the manufacturer.

ingredients.definition: Gelatin (E441) is a protein extracted from animal collagen. Used as a gelling agent in candy, marshmallows, gummies, capsules, yogurt, and many desserts.

common_sources.name: Porcine (pig skin and bone)
common_sources.note: Haram. Most common commercial source in EU and US.

common_sources.name: Bovine (cow hide and bone)
common_sources.note: Halal only if from a halal-slaughtered animal

halal.explanation: Gelatin is classified as mushbooh because the source is rarely declared on the label. The default commercial source in EU and US markets is porcine, which is haram. Bovine and fish gelatin exist but are usually labeled when used. Treat as haram unless the product carries a halal certification.

ingredients.definition: Lard — rendered fat from pork. Used as a cooking fat and as an ingredient in pastries, refried beans, and traditional European baking.

halal.explanation: Lard is rendered fat from pork and is forbidden under the general prohibition of pork and its derivatives.

ingredients.definition: Lecithin (E322) is an emulsifier used to blend oils and water in chocolate, baked goods, margarine, and many processed foods.

common_sources.name: Animal tissue
common_sources.note: Haram if from porcine or non-halal-slaughtered source

halal.explanation: Lecithin is classified as mushbooh because the source is rarely declared on the label. Most commercial lecithin is plant-derived (soybean or sunflower) and halal, but unlabeled lecithin must be verified with the manufacturer. Soy lecithin and sunflower lecithin are halal; lecithin from non-halal animal sources is haram.

names: Mono- and Diglycerides of Fatty Acids
ingredients.definition: Mono- and Diglycerides of Fatty Acids (E471) is an emulsifier derived from glycerol and fatty acids. Used to blend oils and water in bread, margarine, ice cream, peanut butter, and many processed foods.

common_sources.name: Beef tallow
common_sources.note: Halal only if from a halal-slaughtered animal

halal.explanation: Classified as mushbooh because the source fat is almost never declared on the label. Many manufacturers use plant oils (halal), but animal fats including lard are also used. Verification with the manufacturer is the only way to know.

ingredients.definition: Natural Flavoring is a regulatory umbrella term for any flavoring derived from a natural source — plant, animal, or microbial. The label does not name the underlying ingredients.

common_sources.name: Animal-derived (incl. pork, non-halal-slaughtered)
common_sources.note: Mushbooh or haram depending on source

common_sources.name: Alcohol-based extraction (carrier)
common_sources.note: Disputed; many scholars treat any added ethanol as haram

halal.explanation: "Natural flavoring" is an opaque ingredient category that can hide animal products, alcohol-extracted compounds, or other haram inputs. Classified as mushbooh by default; the only way to know is to ask the manufacturer.

ingredients.definition: Natural vanilla flavor is a flavoring made from vanilla beans, typically extracted using ethyl alcohol as the carrier. The label term is regulated but does not specify the solvent.

halal.explanation: Natural vanilla flavor is tricky: the vanilla bean itself is halal, but commercial 'natural vanilla flavor' is usually extracted using ethyl alcohol as a solvent or carrier. Halal status depends on the alcohol amount, the cooking process, and your school of jurisprudence. Conservative ruling: mushbooh.

ingredients.definition: Pork — meat from pigs and any product derived from pigs (bacon, ham, prosciutto, salami, pancetta).

halal.explanation: Pork and all pork derivatives are explicitly forbidden in Islam by direct scriptural ruling.

ingredients.definition: Rennet is an enzyme complex used to coagulate milk into cheese curds. Traditionally extracted from the stomach lining of young ruminant animals.

common_sources.name: Calf stomach (traditional)
common_sources.note: Halal only if from a halal-slaughtered animal

common_sources.name: Adult cow / sheep stomach
common_sources.note: Halal only if from a halal-slaughtered animal

halal.explanation: When a product simply lists "rennet" without qualifying it, the default assumption is animal rennet from a non-halal-slaughtered animal — mushbooh. Microbial and vegetable rennet are halal but are typically labeled as such when used.

opinions.note: Animal vs microbial source must be verified

ingredients.definition: Salt — sodium chloride. A mineral used as a seasoning and preservative.

halal.explanation: Pure salt is a mineral with no animal or alcohol involvement in production. Halal under all schools.

ingredients.definition: Soy Lecithin (E322) — plant-derived emulsifier extracted from soybeans. Used to blend oils and water in chocolate, baked goods, and processed foods.

halal.explanation: Soy lecithin is plant-derived with no animal or alcohol involvement. Halal under all schools.

ingredients.definition: Sugar — sucrose extracted from sugar cane or sugar beets, used as a sweetener.

common_sources.name: Sugar cane (often bone-char filtered in US)
common_sources.note: Halal — bone char is a filter, not an ingredient

halal.explanation: Plant-derived sweetener. Standard production is halal. A small number of scholars discuss bone-char filtration of US cane sugar; the consensus is that the resulting sugar contains no animal matter and is halal as a consumed product.

ingredients.definition: Vanilla is the dried fruit (bean/pod) of an orchid in the genus Vanilla, the source of all vanilla flavoring.

halal.explanation: The vanilla bean itself is a plant product and unambiguously halal. (Vanilla EXTRACT and natural vanilla FLAVOR are separate entries because they typically use alcohol as a solvent.)

ingredients.definition: Vanilla Extract is a flavoring made by macerating vanilla beans in ethanol — typically 35% alcohol by volume per US FDA standard.

halal.explanation: Standard vanilla extract is made with ethanol as the extraction carrier. Many scholars treat any added ethanol as haram regardless of concentration; others permit trace residues from extraction. Alcohol-free vanilla flavoring is unambiguously halal but is rarely the default on labels.

opinions.note: Alcohol carrier; views differ on trace amounts

ingredients.definition: Water — h2O. Used as an ingredient, solvent, and base in nearly all processed foods and beverages.

halal.explanation: Water is halal under all schools.

ingredients.definition: Whey — liquid byproduct of cheese-making, often dried into powder for protein supplements, infant formula, and processed foods.

common_sources.name: Cheese made with halal-slaughtered animal rennet
Cheese made with non-halal animal rennet

halal.explanation: Whey is the byproduct of cheese-making, and the cheese was usually made with rennet. If animal rennet from a non-halal-slaughtered animal was used, the resulting whey inherits the mushbooh status. Whey from microbial-rennet cheese is halal but is rarely labeled that way.

ingredients.definition: Wine — fermented grape juice. An alcoholic beverage and, in cooking wine form, an ingredient in sauces, marinades, and stews.

common_sources.name: Cooking wine
common_sources.note: Haram even if alcohol burns off during cooking

common_sources.name: Non-alcoholic wine vinegar
common_sources.note: Disputed; some scholars permit if the alcohol has fully converted to acetic acid

halal.explanation: Wine is an intoxicant and is explicitly forbidden, including cooking wine and wine added to sauces. The general principle is that any wine added to a product makes that product haram.
